content.text: Every HTTP call an agent makes carries three distinct layers of information:
that describe the request's metadata and capabilities,
that refine which resource or view is requested, and a
that holds the substantive payload — the messages, tool arguments, or binary data.
Getting these three layers right is the difference between an API call that succeeds and one that fails with a cryptic 400 or 415 error, even when the URL and HTTP method are both correct.
An HTTP transaction consists of a
sent by the client and a
returned by the server. Both are structured messages divided into the same three regions:
(a block of key-value pairs), and an optional
(raw bytes). Query parameters are technically part of the URL's start-line, but they function as a separate logical layer for passing lightweight filter criteria.

are key-value pairs separated by a colon that travel before the body in every request and response. They communicate metadata that the server or client needs before it touches the payload: what format the body is in, how to authenticate the caller, which encodings are acceptable, and how to route or trace the request. Common request headers include
appear in the URL after a

. They are visible in the browser address bar, stored in server logs, and safe to bookmark. Their role is to let the same endpoint return different subsets of data: pagination offsets, search terms, result limits, and output format toggles. The library or HTTP client percent-encodes special characters automatically when you pass a dict of params.

(also called the request entity or payload body) is an arbitrary byte stream that follows the headers, separated by a blank line. It carries the actual domain data — for LLM APIs this is almost always a JSON object containing the model name, message list, and generation parameters. For file ingestion tools it may be a multipart form. For some streaming APIs it is newline-delimited JSON or Server-Sent Events in the response direction. A body is optional: GET and DELETE requests conventionally omit it.

Agentic systems call external APIs on every loop iteration. A single misconfigured header or body encoding causes every tool call to fail, not just intermittently — the error is structural and systematic. Three failure categories dominate agent HTTP bugs:
— the server returns 401 or 403 and the agent loop halts or retries uselessly, burning rate-limit quota.
— the server sees the body as form-encoded text when the agent sent JSON, or vice-versa, and returns 400 Bad Request or silently parses garbage fields.
and hammers the API until it gets a 429 ban, making the overall task fail non-deterministically.
LLM APIs in particular require a precise header contract. The Anthropic API and OpenAI API both mandate
. For streaming completions the client must also send

or the server may return the entire completion buffered rather than streaming, defeating the purpose. Tool-calling agents typically pass tool arguments in the JSON body, not as query parameters, because bodies are structured and not URL-length constrained.
Beyond correctness, headers are the primary mechanism for
in distributed agent systems. Correlation headers like

thread a single agent run through every downstream service call, making it possible to reconstruct a full execution timeline from disparate logs. Without them, debugging a multi-hop agent failure means guessing which log lines belong to which run.
— control information (auth, format, routing) lives in headers; domain data lives in the body; filtering criteria live in query params. Each layer can evolve independently.
— the client declares what formats it can consume via
and what it is sending via
; the server selects the best representation. This is how a single endpoint can return JSON to one client and CSV to another.

— GET requests with query parameters are inherently safe and idempotent; reverse proxies and CDNs can cache them by URL, improving throughput for read-heavy agent workflows such as knowledge-base lookups.

— every request is self-contained: the auth token, content type, and all parameters are present in the message itself without server-side session state, enabling horizontal scaling behind load balancers.
— correlation IDs, idempotency keys, and version tokens travel with every request, making distributed agent traces possible without modifying the domain payload.
— browsers and proxies typically cap URLs at around 2 000–8 000 characters; message bodies have no such limit, allowing agents to send multi-turn conversation histories without truncation.

— HTTP/1.1 proxies and servers often cap total header size at 8 KB–16 KB. Accumulating many custom headers or large cookie values can exceed this and cause 431 Request Header Fields Too Large errors that are hard to diagnose.

— query parameters appear in access logs, browser history, and Referer headers. An API key or token passed as a query param is effectively public to anyone with log access. Always use headers for credentials.
— special characters in query values must be percent-encoded. Libraries handle this automatically, but manually constructed URLs with unencoded
inside values silently break parameter parsing.
is absent or wrong, the server may misparse the body entirely and return a success response with no useful error, hiding the real problem for minutes during debugging.
No body on GET in some stacks
— while technically allowed by HTTP/1.1, some proxies, CDNs, and servers strip bodies from GET requests. Agents that try to filter with a GET body will see inconsistent behavior across environments.
— SSE and chunked-transfer responses require the client to maintain a persistent connection, parse line-delimited frames, and handle partial writes. A simple

When a client opens a TCP (or TLS) connection and sends an HTTP/1.1 request, the wire format is plain text: a start-line, zero or more header lines, a blank line (CRLF CRLF), and an optional body. The blank line is the mandatory delimiter — everything before it is headers, everything after is body.

POST /v1/messages HTTP/1.1
Host: api.anthropic.com
Authorization: Bearer sk-ant-api03-...
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 147
anthropic-version: 2023-06-01
X-Request-ID: run-42a1bc

{"model":"claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022","max_tokens":1024,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Summarize this document."}]}

HTTP/2 compresses headers into HPACK binary frames and multiplexes multiple streams over one connection, but the logical structure is identical. Libraries expose the same Python/JavaScript API regardless of protocol version.
Query parameters are appended to the path component of the URL. A library like

accepts them as a dict and percent-encodes them automatically. The resulting URL is deterministic and can be reconstructed from the dict alone, which is why query params are safe for caching and logging.

import httpx

# Library builds: GET /documents?q=agent+memory&limit=10&page=2
resp = httpx.get(
    "https://api.example.com/documents",
    params={"q": "agent memory", "limit": 10, "page": 2},
)
print(resp.request.url)
# https://api.example.com/documents?q=agent+memory&limit=10&page=2
The encoding rules are defined in RFC 3986. Spaces become
in standard percent-encoding. Special characters like
inside values must be encoded as
respectively, otherwise they are parsed as structural delimiters.
HTTP header names are case-insensitive by specification:
are the same header. HTTP/2 requires lowercase names on the wire. Python's
both expose a case-insensitive dict for response headers, so
header tells the server how to interpret the bytes in the body. For LLM APIs the value is always
where the boundary is a unique string the client generates that separates fields within the body. Using the library's
keyword arguments sets the correct Content-Type automatically and handles serialization — never set both manually and via keyword arg simultaneously.
Most LLM APIs return rate-limit information in response headers. The Anthropic API returns

on every response. An agent that reads these headers can implement proactive throttling — sleeping before the next request if remaining drops below a threshold — rather than waiting for a 429 to trigger exponential backoff.

The diagram below shows the full anatomy of an HTTP request-response cycle as an agent experiences it. The three request layers (URL + query params, headers, body) and two response layers (response headers, response body) are highlighted independently so their distinct roles are clear.

attrs.alt: HTTP request anatomy diagram showing the three request layers and two response layers with an agent client on the left and API server on the right

content.text: The key architectural insight is that each layer has a different visibility profile:
query params are in the URL
and appear in every log, CDN cache key, and browser history entry;
visible to every hop in the network path (proxies, load balancers) but not in the URL itself; and
that only the terminal server and end client see in detail. This profile determines where secrets, filters, and payloads belong.
— always pass API keys, tokens, and session credentials in the
header, never in query params or the body where possible.
on requests and check it on responses before parsing.
when opening an SSE stream so the server knows not to buffer the response.
and idempotency keys on mutating operations to enable deduplication and distributed tracing.
select API behavior without URL versioning.
— pass filter criteria that narrow the returned resource set.
array, system prompt, tool definitions, and generation parameters all go in the JSON body.
— POST/PUT/PATCH bodies carry the new state or delta for the resource being created or modified.
— use multipart form data for binary content; never base64-encode large files inside a JSON body when the API supports native upload.
— complex nested objects and arrays that would be unwieldy as query params.
Do NOT use query params for
Secrets or credentials of any kind — they appear in logs and Referer headers.
Data that changes frequently without being bookmarkable — the URL should represent a stable resource.
Large structured inputs — URL length limits (typically 2 000–8 000 characters) make this impractical.
The recommended pattern for agentic applications is to create a thin

that centralizes header construction, sets a base URL, configures timeouts, and exposes higher-level methods (one per API operation). This avoids scattering header logic across dozens of tool-call sites.
lives in one place so a token rotation only needs to touch the constructor;
lets httpx handle serialization and Content-Type;
handles query-parameter encoding; and rate-limit headers are read before raising the status so the caller can log warnings even on success.
Streaming completions require a different pattern because the response body arrives in chunks over time. The
header signals intent to the server, and the client iterates over lines of the open connection.
Example 1 — Basic LLM API call with all required headers (Python)

import httpx
import os

api_key = os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]

headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}",
    "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
}

body = {
    "model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
    "max_tokens": 256,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is 12 * 8?"}],
}

resp = httpx.post(
    "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages",
    headers=headers,
    json=body,
    timeout=30.0,
)
resp.raise_for_status()
data = resp.json()
print(data["content"][0]["text"])  # 96
Example 2 — Paginated list query with query parameters (JavaScript)
Example 3 — Multipart file upload with mixed headers

import httpx

def ingest_pdf(base_url: str, api_key: str, pdf_path: str) -> dict:
    """Upload a PDF to a document-ingestion API using multipart form data."""
    with open(pdf_path, "rb") as f:
        pdf_bytes = f.read()

    # httpx sets Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=<auto>
    # when 'files=' is used — do NOT also set Content-Type manually
    resp = httpx.post(
        f"{base_url}/ingest",
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}",
            "Accept": "application/json",
            "X-Request-ID": "ingest-001",
        },
        files={"file": (pdf_path.split("/")[-1], pdf_bytes, "application/pdf")},
        data={"strategy": "fast", "chunk_size": "512"},  # form fields alongside file
        timeout=120.0,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

Consider an agentic document analysis pipeline that processes user-uploaded PDFs: the agent first ingests each file via multipart upload (body carries bytes), then queries the vector store using search terms extracted from the document (query params carry filters), and finally calls the LLM to synthesize findings (JSON body carries the assembled context and messages). Each step uses a different request layer correctly.

import httpx
import uuid


class DocAnalysisAgent:
    def __init__(self, api_key: str, vector_store_url: str, llm_url: str):
        self._key = api_key
        self._vs_url = vector_store_url
        self._llm_url = llm_url
        self._run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())

    def _headers(self, content_type: str = "application/json") -> dict:
        return {
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self._key}",
            "Content-Type": content_type,
            "Accept": "application/json",
            "X-Run-ID": self._run_id,
        }

    def ingest(self, pdf_bytes: bytes, filename: str) -> str:
        """Upload PDF; returns document ID."""
        resp = httpx.post(
            f"{self._vs_url}/documents",
            headers={
                "Authorization": f"Bearer {self._key}",
                "Accept": "application/json",
                "X-Run-ID": self._run_id,
            },
            files={"file": (filename, pdf_bytes, "application/pdf")},
            timeout=60,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()["id"]

    def search(self, doc_id: str, query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
        """Retrieve relevant chunks — filters via query params."""
        resp = httpx.get(
            f"{self._vs_url}/search",
            params={"doc_id": doc_id, "q": query, "top_k": top_k},
            headers=self._headers(),
            timeout=15,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        # Check rate-limit headroom proactively
        remaining = resp.headers.get("x-ratelimit-remaining")
        if remaining and int(remaining) < 3:
            import time; time.sleep(1)
        return resp.json()["chunks"]

    def synthesize(self, chunks: list[dict], question: str) -> str:
        """Call LLM with assembled context in JSON body."""
        context = "\n\n".join(c["text"] for c in chunks)
        body = {
            "model": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
            "max_tokens": 1024,
            "system": "Answer questions using only the provided context.",
            "messages": [
                {"role": "user", "content": f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"},
            ],
        }
        resp = httpx.post(
            f"{self._llm_url}/v1/messages",
            headers=self._headers(),
            json=body,
            timeout=60,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()["content"][0]["text"]

This pattern — one API client class per service, trace IDs on every request, content-type checked on response, rate-limit headers read proactively — is the production-grade idiom used in real agentic pipelines at scale. The
header threads a single agent execution through three different backend services, making it trivial to filter logs for a specific run.
Passing an already-serialized JSON string to the
keyword argument causes double-encoding: the string gets serialized again as a JSON string literal, and the server receives a string rather than an object.

# WRONG — json= serializes again, server gets a quoted string
body_str = json.dumps({"model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", "messages": [...]})
resp = httpx.post(url, json=body_str, headers=headers)  # double-encoded!

# RIGHT — pass the dict directly; httpx serializes it once
body = {"model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", "messages": [...]}
resp = httpx.post(url, json=body, headers=headers)
Mistake 2 — Setting Content-Type manually when using files=

# WRONG — manually setting Content-Type breaks the boundary parameter
headers["Content-Type"] = "multipart/form-data"  # missing boundary!
resp = httpx.post(url, headers=headers, files={"file": (name, data, "application/pdf")})

# RIGHT — let httpx set Content-Type automatically when using files=
headers_no_ct = {k: v for k, v in headers.items() if k != "Content-Type"}
resp = httpx.post(url, headers=headers_no_ct, files={"file": (name, data, "application/pdf")})
Mistake 3 — Putting secrets in query params

# WRONG — api_key appears in server logs, CDN logs, browser history
resp = httpx.get(f"https://api.example.com/data?api_key={api_key}&limit=10")

# RIGHT — credentials go in the Authorization header
resp = httpx.get(
    "https://api.example.com/data",
    params={"limit": 10},
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"},
)
Mistake 4 — Not reading Content-Type before parsing

# WRONG — crashes if server returns HTML error page instead of JSON
data = resp.json()

# RIGHT — check Content-Type first; log the raw text on mismatch
ct = resp.headers.get("content-type", "")
if "application/json" in ct:
    data = resp.json()
else:
    raise ValueError(f"Unexpected content-type {ct!r}: {resp.text[:200]}")

# WRONG — hammers until 429, then backs off reactively
for task in tasks:
    resp = httpx.post(url, json=task, headers=headers)
    resp.raise_for_status()

# RIGHT — proactive throttling using response headers
import time
for task in tasks:
    resp = httpx.post(url, json=task, headers=headers)
    remaining = int(resp.headers.get("x-ratelimit-remaining-requests", 100))
    reset_ms = float(resp.headers.get("x-ratelimit-reset-requests", 0))
    if remaining < 3 and reset_ms > 0:
        time.sleep(reset_ms / 1000.0)
    resp.raise_for_status()
— put all headers in a single method on an API client class. Rotating a key, adding a new version header, or inserting a tracer needs to happen in exactly one place.
Use the library's json= and params= kwargs
; never build query strings by string concatenation. Library kwargs handle encoding, escaping, and Content-Type correctly.
— both a connect timeout and a read timeout. LLM streaming calls can last many seconds; a missing read timeout leaves threads hung indefinitely if the server drops the connection mid-stream.
— APIs under load sometimes return HTML error pages or gateway timeouts with status 200. A Content-Type check before calling
surfaces the real error instead of a JSON parse failure.
Attach correlation IDs to every outbound request
— generate a run-level UUID at agent startup and thread it through all downstream calls as
. This is the minimum viable observability for multi-hop agents.
— implement a thin wrapper that checks
after every response and sleeps until the reset timestamp if headroom is low. This prevents cascading 429 failures in tight agent loops.
— configure structured loggers to redact the
headers before writing to any output. Treat them like passwords because they are.
Use idempotency keys on POST mutations
for operations that create or modify resources. If the agent retries after a network timeout, the server will return the original result instead of duplicating the operation.
Opening a new TCP/TLS connection for every HTTP request adds 50–300 ms of latency and burns file descriptors. Use a persistent HTTP client (

) that maintains a connection pool across requests to the same host. With HTTP/2, the pool can multiplex dozens of concurrent agent requests over a single connection — especially valuable when calling LLM and tool APIs in parallel during fan-out steps.

HTTP/1.1 sends headers as uncompressed plain text on every request. Adding twenty custom headers per call to a high-throughput agent can meaningfully increase the fraction of bandwidth consumed by overhead rather than payload. HTTP/2's HPACK compression eliminates most of this cost by maintaining a header table that avoids re-sending repeated headers. Prefer
Parallel tool calls and body size

Agent frameworks often execute multiple tool calls concurrently. Each concurrent call needs its own trace header with a unique request ID (shared run ID is fine, but the per-request ID must differ). Large JSON bodies — for example, a messages array containing embedded image data — can cause latency spikes if the server's ingest buffer is small. Compress with
for very large bodies (libraries like httpx handle this with
plus the header) or consider chunked transfer encoding.

For long LLM completions, streaming responses (SSE) reduce time-to-first-token dramatically and keep the agent interactive. However, streaming consumes one connection per active call for the full duration. An agent making 10 simultaneous streaming calls to an LLM holds 10 persistent connections. Size the connection pool and set appropriate keep-alive timeouts to avoid exhaustion. For non-interactive batch jobs, buffered responses are simpler to handle and more compressible.
— modern async/sync client with HTTP/2 support, timeout API, and type annotations. Preferred for new agentic code. Install:
— ubiquitous synchronous client; no HTTP/2 support; adequate for simple scripts. Install:
— high-performance async client popular in async-first frameworks. Install:
— available in Node 18+ and all browsers; no install needed; returns a streaming ReadableStream for SSE.
— adds request/response interceptors, automatic JSON parsing, and cancellation. Useful for adding correlation headers globally.
— feature-rich Node.js client with retry, pagination helpers, and hook system.
— CLI tool that pretty-prints headers and bodies; excellent for manually testing API endpoints.
— the lowest-level debugger; prints raw request and response headers.
— intercept and inspect all HTTP traffic from an agent process; invaluable for debugging what headers a third-party library actually sends.
— register request and response hooks on an httpx client to log all headers without modifying call sites.
Different mechanisms exist for passing data in an HTTP request. The choice affects security, cacheability, and size limits.
Visible in logs and history — never use for secrets
Low — not in URL; redact in logs
Visible in logs — fine for non-secret IDs
Subject to CSRF if not protected
Low — but loses structure vs. JSON
API keys and bearer tokens belong exclusively in the
for services that use that convention). They must never appear in query params, request bodies, or log output. Configure structured loggers to redact these headers by name. In Python's
module or cloud logging SDKs, a filter can strip them before the record is serialized.

Always enforce HTTPS in production agents — headers travel in plaintext over unencrypted HTTP, exposing Authorization tokens on any shared network. When constructing header values from user-supplied input, be aware of header injection: a newline character (
) in a header value allows an attacker to inject additional headers or split the HTTP message. Use libraries that validate header values (
or header-validation errors on injection attempts).

Agents commonly retry on transient failures (network timeouts, 503s). For POST endpoints that create resources, retrying without an idempotency key can duplicate the operation — creating two bookings, two payments, or two LLM completions. Send a stable UUID in the
header on the first attempt and reuse the same key on retries. The server returns the same response for repeated calls with the same key within a time window.

Streaming LLM responses can last 30–90 seconds. Without an explicit read timeout, a server that drops the connection mid-stream leaves the agent thread blocked indefinitely. Set both a connect timeout and a read timeout. For streaming, set the read timeout to the maximum acceptable gap between chunks rather than the total response time — httpx exposes this as
. Wrap streaming calls in a context manager to guarantee the connection is released on exit or exception.
Validate response headers and status codes defensively. A 200 status with an HTML body (a WAF or CDN error page) can masquerade as a successful API call if the body is not checked. Always confirm
contains the expected MIME type before parsing. Use a schema validation library (
) to validate the parsed JSON against the expected shape — malformed responses from degraded APIs should raise early rather than propagate partial data silently into agent state.
Always put credentials in headers, never in query params
— query params appear in server logs, CDN caches, and browser history; headers do not appear in the URL.
Use the library's json= and params= kwargs
— they handle serialization, percent-encoding, and Content-Type automatically; manual string concatenation or json.dumps inside json= causes double-encoding bugs.
Content-Type and Accept headers are not optional for LLM APIs
— a missing or wrong Content-Type causes the server to misparse the body and return a 400 even when the URL and method are correct.
Read x-ratelimit-remaining from response headers proactively
— sleeping before hitting the limit prevents cascading 429 failures that abort an entire agent run.
Attach a correlation ID (X-Request-ID) to every outbound request
— this is the minimum viable observability for a multi-hop agent: it threads a single execution through distributed service logs.
— degraded APIs and WAFs return HTML error pages with 200 status; a content-type guard surfaces the real problem instead of an opaque JSON parse exception.
Use a persistent HTTP client with a connection pool
— creating a new connection per request adds 50–300 ms of latency per call; connection reuse and HTTP/2 multiplexing are essential for performance-sensitive agent loops.
Set both connect and read timeouts on all requests
— a streaming LLM call without a read timeout can block agent threads indefinitely if the server drops the connection mid-response.
Send Idempotency-Key on POST mutations before retrying
— reusing a stable UUID key prevents duplicate resource creation when the agent retries after a network timeout.
